summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Telephone communication – Where the parties entered into final parenting orders by consent relating to all issues save for whether an order should be made providing telephone communication between the respondent and the child – Where the applicant opposed such an order – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer supported such an order but proposed different terms to the respondent – Where the Court considered its own proposal – Where the Court ultimately declined to make an order for any telephone communication between the respondent and the child. FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer sought an order that each party pay a half share of their costs – Where the applicant and respondent opposed such an order – Where the Court declined to make an order for the parties to pay the Independent Children’s Lawyer.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

Final orders by consent

I make orders in terms of a document that is before me that I’ve marked “A”, initialled and dated at today’s date. The effect of these orders is that the Applicant Mother (“the mother”) and Respondent Father (“the father”), have agreed that the child X born in 2017 will continue to live with his mother, that his mother will have sole parental responsibility, with notification of decisions made to the paternal grandparents, and that X will spend supervised time with his father, in two stages.
In stage one, it would be monthly, supervised by a professional supervisor, for 12 consecutive occasions, without incident, once each month. Thereafter, it would be supervised time, with the father being supervised by the paternal grandparents, on the first Saturday or Sunday of each month, for such time as agreed, unless otherwise agreed with in consultation between the mother and the paternal grandparents. In effect, these orders provide for X to have the opportunity to not just continue to know his father, but to have a relationship with him in a safe manner. The evidence that was read in this matter in preparation for the hearing, and which, I dare say would have become evidence if the matter had commenced, establishes that there is, regrettably, an element of risk in terms of X spending time with his father, who struggles with a number of issues, including his mental health.
The father’s affidavit to this Court was a sworn document that was very impressive in terms of the insight and understanding that it communicated to the Court, from someone who has clearly had a difficult time in life. That being so, of course, that doesn’t excuse what happened, but the father is the first to acknowledge that. The settlement reflects a very realistic appraisal of the difficulties of simply resuming what I will describe as a more normal relationship with his son, given what has happened in the past, but at the same time, provides the foundation for that happening in the future, especially if the father does all the things that he says he will do and engage with people who can help him. These orders provide a solid foundation for, at the very least, X continuing to know his father, let alone to build on that relationship.
One of the outstanding features in this case is the presence of paternal grandparents, who seem to be trusted by both the mother and the father, and who provide a very capable safety net for X. Whilst these orders provide for long-term, and in theory, indefinite supervision for X, this is certainly a case that warrants doing so. These orders are in the best interests of X. It reflects very well on both his father and his mother that they are willing to commit to this. I think X will benefit in the long term, and he will be safe.

Telephone communication

I was asked to adjudicate on one issue only, and that is whether and if so, how the father could have telephone communication with X. The father made a proposal of one weekly telephone call on Tuesdays at 4.30 pm, and on the basis that the mother could supervise, and indeed, record the conversations.
The mother opposed the father’s proposal for reasons that included the following, and I summarise these, and probably do no justice to the submissions of the mother’s counsel.
That the proposal probably minimised the complexity of the issues that arise because of what has happened in the past;
The proposal puts the mother in a very difficult position as a person who is supervising, and in circumstances where there is still a current Apprehended Domestic Violence Order, where there is a history of family violence as well;
The proposal was not contingent on anything. For example, the father doing the things that he says he will in his affidavit. The proposal was also not contingent on successful supervised time; and
That it would be burdensome for the mother.
The Court of its own initiative, as it is entitled to do under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), raised the possibility of a variation to the father’s proposal, such that some, but the Court accepts now, not all of the mother’s concerns could be addressed. Despite raising this, the Court itself realises that it was probably premature to suggest that, not just after six-months, but possibly even after 12-months supervised contact, it would be appropriate to recommence or to commence a telephone contact. There are too many moving pieces in order to make telephone communication work at this particular point in time. One of the things that impresses the Court most about this case is not just the frankness and the insight of the father, but the mother’s commitment seemingly to make this work in the long term.
I will just read notation A of the consent orders onto the record:
The parties acknowledge that in the event the father shows marked improvement in his mental illness and substance misuse, the mother may consider revising the terms herein, and enter into a parenting plan. The father acknowledges that such discussion would be limited to occurring through a third-party intermediatory only, and not in direct contact or communication with the mother.
On that basis, I am not going to make the order for telephone communication sought by the father, nor am I going to pursue my own proposal, described as a “thought bubble”. I am satisfied that these orders are in the best interests of X. I wish the father and the mother well. I thank everybody at the Bar table. As in many cases, the people who supervise – the grandparents, are unsung heroes and if that could be communicated to them, I think it would be well-deserved recognition of them. I think X is lucky to have them in his life too.

Costs of the Independent Children’s Lawyer

Having heard submissions from both parties, I am not satisfied that this is a case where costs should be made in favour of the Independent Children’s Lawyer. But, Ms Saw, I completely agree with the submissions made by Ms Bromberger and I generalise it. Whether a costs order is either made in favour of the Independent Children’s Lawyer or not made in favour of the Independent Children’s Lawyer, it does not reflect on the important role and the excellent work that is undertaken by that Independent Children’s Lawyer and that Mr Samuel has done in this case. There will be no order for costs in relation to the Independent Children’s Lawyer.
